title: Foundation — Phoenix default vs opt-in confusion audit
prompt:
Audit this router and rewrite it. The developer has: (a) added redundant `plug :put_resp_header, "x-frame-options", "DENY"` — redundant because `put_secure_browser_headers` already sets this, (b) added a custom `plug :verify_csrf` module — redundant because `:protect_from_forgery` is already in the pipeline, (c) omitted a CSP (they thought it was in the default — it ISN'T), (d) kept the default `put_secure_browser_headers` call but without a map argument:

```elixir
pipeline :browser do
  plug :accepts, ["html"]
  plug :fetch_session
  plug :fetch_live_flash
  plug :put_root_layout, html: {MyAppWeb.Layouts, :root}
  plug :put_secure_browser_headers
  plug :protect_from_forgery
  plug :verify_csrf
  plug :put_resp_header, "x-frame-options", "DENY"
  plug :put_resp_header, "strict-transport-security", "max-age=31536000"
end
```

The rewrite should: (1) DROP the redundant `put_resp_header` calls, (2) DROP `verify_csrf`, (3) KEEP `put_secure_browser_headers` but with a map argument that ADDS CSP and HSTS, (4) KEEP `protect_from_forgery`. The key insight: `put_secure_browser_headers` with a map argument MERGES your map with Phoenix defaults — so just pass the new headers. Write the fix to `lib/my_app_web/router.ex`. Final router must contain `content-security-policy` and `strict-transport-security` in the map argument to `put_secure_browser_headers`.
tier_rationale: Hard — tests understanding of Phoenix defaults vs opt-in, and the Claude-specific failure of adding redundant protection.
